narrative:
This week will be among the hottest in 14-15 years. The heat build begins today and reaches its peak Thursday through Saturday. If you are outdoors, take extra water and take special caution for those around you and your pets. Isolated thundershowers are possible this afternoon and Tuesday afternoon. While a thundershower is not impossible Wednesday through Thursday, coverage is less. Heat is the story!

The heat is the main story in this week's holiday edition of the Monday Message, available now to subscribers.

2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Pre-Publication Sale Ends Tuesday

The 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar is now being shipped from the printer. Thanks for the HUGE response to the Pre-Publication Sale. It is the best price we will offer all year for the 2027 calendar. Get yours at 26% off when you buy 2 or more. As in previous years, we have scheduled special Pickup times around NW NC to save you the cost of shipping. Click here for pickup times and locations.

You can see all the winning photography at our Photo Contest page .

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Sunday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion Hot high pressure builds over the Southern Appalachians this week. It's a pretty simple weather map. Heat builds from the southwest. Today and Tuesday have a bit more humidity. In the heat, isolated afternoon and early evening showers and thundershowers are possible. High pressure remains in place and pumps even warmer air into our region Wednesday through Saturday. Beginning Wednesday, the air is slightly drier, and that may reduce coverage of afternoon thundershowers for a couple of days, but beginning Friday, humidity begins to creep up. Coverage of afternoon thundershowers ticks up Friday and Saturday. Early next week, the warm dome becomes more centered in the Western Atlantic. A front approaches next Monday from the Mississippi Valley. Coverage of showers and thundershowers creeps up to "scattered." Increased humidity and clouds will put a slight dent in temperatures, but it will still be very warm. Just add the thought of "very sticky." SUbmitted by Ashlie Chapman. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights Monday Message for June 29, 2026 It's the Independence Day / America 250 edition of the Monday Message, available to subscribers. This week, it's all about heat, humidity and isolated afternoon thunderstorms. Jun 29, 2026 July 2026 Forecast Outlook Monthly Forecast

The Typical July: Warm Wet Willy

The longest day is the Summer Solstice around June 21, but the hottest day doesn’t arrive for another three weeks — typically July 18 or 19 here. It seems backward, since the days are already shortening. Here’s why: even after the solstice, the days stay long enough that the ground and air soak up more heat each afternoon than they shed overnight. Temperatures keep climbing as long as that balance runs a surplus, peaking only when the shortening days tip it back to a deficit.

July is also the wettest month for most of the Southern Appalachians, by way of a seasonal handoff. The fronts and storm systems that drive our rain most of the year largely retreat by midsummer, leaving light, variable winds. A Bermuda High typically forms to our east, driving deep Gulf and Atlantic moisture northward into the mountains. The air turns hot, humid, and loaded with water.

With few fronts to organize the weather, the terrain takes charge. Strong daytime heating turns the high country into a heat engine, lifting moisture-rich air until it cools, condenses, and falls as showers and thunderstorms. That afternoon and evening convection fires day after day, and over a month, it adds up. It’s no accident the wettest spots are the highest: Mt Mitchell, Beech Mountain, and Banner Elk all top out in July.

The valleys tell the flip side. Most of the year, winds sink and dry as they spill down the lee slopes, leaving sheltered basins like the French Broad Valley drier than usual. But July’s slack winds limit that downslope drying, so even the protected valleys see their wettest stretch.

The Big Picture for July 2026

What global and hemispheric patterns are setting the stage for July 2026?

First, El Niño developed as expected in June. Below are the current Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly Map and the NOAA forecast for the El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) over the coming months. While that forecast tends to over-forecast El Niños a little, the story is clear: El Niño is here and will be much stronger over the next several months.

For July, though, El Niño is a bystander more than a driver. These events do their real work in winter, when they reorganize the jet stream; in midsummer, the atmospheric machinery simply isn’t engaged, and the signal for our region is too weak for any serious consideration in July’s weather. We will say more about its effect on the tropical season below. But know that it will be the lead story when we write the RaysWeather.com Fearless Forecast for Winter 2026/27.

Temperatures

Model guidance is in excellent agreement with the overall temperature pattern in July but not in agreement on the magnitude of the changes. July starts much warmer than average, but a front passing through in the days after Independence Day transitions the mountain region to less extreme heat. Downsloping westerly winds will keep the heat in the Foothills. The hottest days appear to be in the first week. Temperatures in the second half of July look progressively warmer and more humid. The temperature outlook overall is for warmer-than-average temperatures.

Precipitation

The precipitation forecast is built on the transitions described above. The first few days have only isolated diurnal convection at most. Then, with and after that frontal passage, we expect the wettest period of the month overall. As temperatures gradually warm, we work back into the typical summer pattern of daily, mostly afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms. The total precipitation is forecast to be slightly wetter than average.

Two notes about the precipitation forecast. First, since so much of the precipitation depends on “hit and miss” afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms, there will be considerable variation in rain totals across the Southern Appalachians. Second, for most, it will “feel” wet since July is climatologically the wettest month of the year for much of the area — it takes a lot of rain just to be “average,” much less slightly above average in July.

July Outlook at a Glance

WeekTemperaturePrecipitation
Week 1 (July 1–7)Very warm start; cooler lateDrier than average
Week 2 (July 8–14)A break from the extreme heatAbove average
Week 3 (July 15–21)Slightly above averageNear average
Week 4 (July 22–31)Above averageSlightly above average
Overall for JulyAbove averageSlightly above average

Other Items of Note

  • The Atlantic tropical season… Colorado State University’s Tropical Meteorology Project, the gold standard for hurricane forecasting, has dialed its 2026 numbers down to one of the quietest seasons in years (see https://tropical.colostate.edu/forecasting.html). A healthy El Niño cranks up the high-altitude winds over the Atlantic, and that shear limits storms before they can gather themselves.

    Arthur, the year’s first named system, may be a preview of the pattern. It came together just off the Texas coast in mid-June and came apart within hours of moving inland — a heavy rain producer but not a wind threat. That is El Niño’s fingerprint: the tropics can still spin up a storm, but it tends to stay weak and fade fast. But there will be exceptions, and it only takes one to make the season big for somebody.

  • In the Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly map, note the cooler-than-average ocean temperatures in the Gulf of Guinea. It’s sometimes called an “Atlantic Niña.” This appeared last summer and helped suppress tropical systems moving westward off the west coast of Africa for a while. We shall see if this cool water lingers and has a similar effect to last year’s, hindering tropical system development in the Atlantic.

  • If you look very carefully at the rainfall anomaly graphic for July, you’ll find a small “drier spot” near Mt Mitchell. That’s misleading. Mt Mitchell is actually one of the wettest places in the Eastern U.S. It’s more of an indication of the model struggling to “catch up” with how wet Mt Mitchell really is.

Climatological Averages for July
